title: Diabetes Insipidus
oneLiner: Disorder of water balance causing polyuria & polydipsia due to ADH deficiency (central) or renal resistance (nephrogenic); can rapidly cause life-threatening hypernatraemia.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe hypernatraemia (Na > 160 mmol/L):** Look for obtundation, seizures, or focal neurology – this is a medical emergency requiring slow controlled correction (≤ 10 mmol/L per 24h). Rapid correction causes cerebral oedema.
- **Hypovolaemic shock from massive free water loss:** Tachycardia, postural hypotension, oliguria (paradoxical in DI because polyuria persists until severe volume depletion). Need urgent isotonic fluid resuscitation before calculating water deficit.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Polyuria (urine output > 40 mL/kg/24h), polydipsia (intense thirst for cold water), nocturia, enuresis in children
- **Key exam findings:** Dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, tachycardia, postural hypotension; hypertonic saline in central DI may have visual field defects or cranial nerve palsies from underlying pituitary lesion
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Primary polydipsia (psychogenic/dipsogenic):** Hyponatraemia or low normal Na; urine osmolality < 300 mOsmol/kg before water deprivation
- **Diabetes mellitus:** Polyuria with glycosuria; hyperglycaemia; urine dipstick glucose 3+/4+
- **Acute kidney injury (non-oliguric):** Rising creatinine, less intense thirst, urine osmolality may be fixed
- **Hypercalcaemia or hypokalaemia:** Check serum Ca and K; polyuria resolves after correction
- **Splitter:** **Plasma osmolality** — if high (> 295 mOsmol/kg) suggests DI; if low (< 280 mOsmol/kg) suggests primary polydipsia
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Urine output chart (strict 24h), urine dipstick (glucose, ketones, specific gravity), serum osmolality (or calculated: 2 × Na + glucose + urea), fluid balance
- **Bloods:** U&Es (critical: Na, K, urea, creatinine), serum osmolality, calcium, glucose, lithium level (if on lithium)
- **Imaging:** **MRI pituitary (with contrast)** — first-line for suspected central DI; look for pituitary stalk thickening, empty sella, tumour or haemorrhage
- **Special tests:** Water deprivation test (see diagnostic criteria) – but **only perform if Na < 150 mmol/L and patient not hypotensive**; otherwise start treatment empirically.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Water deprivation test protocol (after 8h fluid restriction):
  - Urine osmolality < 300 mOsmol/kg + plasma osmolality > 295 mOsmol/kg → DI
  - After desmopressin (DDAVP): urine osmolality rise > 50% → central DI; < 50% → nephrogenic DI
  - Primary polydipsia: urine osmolality > 400 mOsmol/kg after deprivation
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum sodium and plasma osmolality** — omitting them means you cannot correctly manage fluids and risk fatal hypernatraemia or cerebral oedema
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Establish IV access; check Na, K, creatinine, osmolality hourly initially
  - If Na > 160 mmol/L or patient symptomatic (seizures, coma): **start 5% glucose/water IV at 1 mL/kg/h** (do NOT use hypotonic saline) and discuss with ITU/senior. Aim to lower Na by ≤ 10 mmol/L per 24h.
  - If hypovolaemic: resuscitate with **0.9% saline** until haemodynamics stable; then switch to 5% glucose water maintenance
  - For central DI (known/pituitary surgery): give **desmopressin (DDAVP)** SC/IV/PO as per local policy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Strict 4-hourly urine output + fluid balance chart
  - Daily U&Es (more frequent if Na rising or falling rapidly)
  - If central DI: started on desmopressin, titrate to maintain normonatraemia and acceptable urine output (3-4 L/day)
  - If nephrogenic DI: stop offending drug (lithium), correct hypercalcaemia/hypokalaemia, consider thiazide diuretic or amiloride (nephrogenic DI)
  - Ensure free access to water; never restrict fluids in a conscious patient
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Central DI: desmopressin (oral, intranasal, or SC) — dose adjusted to thirst and serum Na
  - Nephrogenic DI: low-salt diet, thiazide diuretic (e.g., bendroflumethiazide 2.5-5 mg daily) ± amiloride
  - Endocrine follow-up for central DI; renal follow-up for nephrogenic
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 5% glucose/water at maintenance rate (e.g., 30 mL/kg/24h) + 0.9% saline for volume losses or if Na stable
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily unless contraindicated (weight, renal function)
  - Diet: normal with unrestricted salt (unless on thiazide)
  - Observations: 4-hourly – BP, HR, weight, urine output; NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (central DI):** Desmopressin (DDAVP) — dose: [check local policy] (typical: oral 100-200mcg TDS; IV/SC 1-2mcg BD; intranasal 10-20mcg daily)
- **Nephrogenic DI:** Thiazide diuretic — bendroflumethiazide 2.5-5 mg PO daily (check local policy); or amiloride 5-10 mg daily if lithium-induced
- **Key contraindications:** Desmopressin: caution in coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyponatraemia; thiazides: anuria, sulfonamide allergy, uncorrected hypokalaemia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Desmopressin – reduce dose in renal impairment; thiazides – avoid in severe renal impairment (eGFR < 30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Over-desmopressin causing hyponatraemia** — check Na daily; never repeat desmopressin without checking urine output and sodium. Signs: headache, nausea, confusion (hyponatraemic encephalopathy)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Severe hypernatraemia → seizures, cerebral oedema, coma; hypovolaemic shock; venous thrombosis from haemoconcentration
- **Chronic:** Hydronephrosis from massive urine flow (uncommon); bladder distension; renal impairment from repeated volume depletion; side effects of desmopressin (hyponatraemia)
sections.Prognosis: - Central DI: excellent with appropriate desmopressin replacement; quality of life good if compliant. Nephrogenic DI: reversible if cause corrected; if chronic (e.g., lithium), may need lifelong thiazide therapy; renal prognosis dependent on underlying condition.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Perform urine osmolality on first void** – baseline result guides water deprivation test decision
- **In any polyuria after pituitary surgery** – give a stat dose of desmopressin and monitor response; don’t wait for formal diagnosis
- **If patient has free access to water – they may correct hypernatraemia themselves** – chart fluid intake accurately
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not restrict water** – this causes rapid Na rise and cerebral injury
- **Do not give desmopressin before ruling out primary polydipsia** – can cause severe hyponatraemia
- **Do not correct hypernatraemia faster than 10 mmol/L per day** – risk of cerebral oedema and herniation
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Unable to maintain oral fluids (vomiting), new confusion, headache, seizure, or urine output > 5 L/day or < 500 mL/day
- **Follow-up:** Endocrine clinic in 2-4 weeks for central DI; renal clinic for nephrogenic DI; monitor serum Na weekly initially until stable
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - Na > 160 mmol/L or < 120 mmol/L
  - Seizures, altered GCS (< 15), focal neurological signs
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or lactate > 2 mmol/L
  - Anuria or oliguria (uncommon in DI)
- **Specific threshold:** GCS < 13 or rapid drop requires immediate senior review and consider ITU for controlled Na correction

rawMarkdown:
## Diabetes Insipidus
**One-liner:** Disorder of water balance causing polyuria & polydipsia due to ADH deficiency (central) or renal resistance (nephrogenic); can rapidly cause life-threatening hypernatraemia.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe hypernatraemia (Na > 160 mmol/L):** Look for obtundation, seizures, or focal neurology – this is a medical emergency requiring slow controlled correction (≤ 10 mmol/L per 24h). Rapid correction causes cerebral oedema.
- **Hypovolaemic shock from massive free water loss:** Tachycardia, postural hypotension, oliguria (paradoxical in DI because polyuria persists until severe volume depletion). Need urgent isotonic fluid resuscitation before calculating water deficit.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Polyuria (urine output > 40 mL/kg/24h), polydipsia (intense thirst for cold water), nocturia, enuresis in children
- **Key exam findings:** Dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, tachycardia, postural hypotension; hypertonic saline in central DI may have visual field defects or cranial nerve palsies from underlying pituitary lesion

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Primary polydipsia (psychogenic/dipsogenic):** Hyponatraemia or low normal Na; urine osmolality < 300 mOsmol/kg before water deprivation
- **Diabetes mellitus:** Polyuria with glycosuria; hyperglycaemia; urine dipstick glucose 3+/4+
- **Acute kidney injury (non-oliguric):** Rising creatinine, less intense thirst, urine osmolality may be fixed
- **Hypercalcaemia or hypokalaemia:** Check serum Ca and K; polyuria resolves after correction
- **Splitter:** **Plasma osmolality** — if high (> 295 mOsmol/kg) suggests DI; if low (< 280 mOsmol/kg) suggests primary polydipsia

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Urine output chart (strict 24h), urine dipstick (glucose, ketones, specific gravity), serum osmolality (or calculated: 2 × Na + glucose + urea), fluid balance
- **Bloods:** U&Es (critical: Na, K, urea, creatinine), serum osmolality, calcium, glucose, lithium level (if on lithium)
- **Imaging:** **MRI pituitary (with contrast)** — first-line for suspected central DI; look for pituitary stalk thickening, empty sella, tumour or haemorrhage
- **Special tests:** Water deprivation test (see diagnostic criteria) – but **only perform if Na < 150 mmol/L and patient not hypotensive**; otherwise start treatment empirically.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Water deprivation test protocol (after 8h fluid restriction):
  - Urine osmolality < 300 mOsmol/kg + plasma osmolality > 295 mOsmol/kg → DI
  - After desmopressin (DDAVP): urine osmolality rise > 50% → central DI; < 50% → nephrogenic DI
  - Primary polydipsia: urine osmolality > 400 mOsmol/kg after deprivation
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum sodium and plasma osmolality** — omitting them means you cannot correctly manage fluids and risk fatal hypernatraemia or cerebral oedema

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Establish IV access; check Na, K, creatinine, osmolality hourly initially
  - If Na > 160 mmol/L or patient symptomatic (seizures, coma): **start 5% glucose/water IV at 1 mL/kg/h** (do NOT use hypotonic saline) and discuss with ITU/senior. Aim to lower Na by ≤ 10 mmol/L per 24h.
  - If hypovolaemic: resuscitate with **0.9% saline** until haemodynamics stable; then switch to 5% glucose water maintenance
  - For central DI (known/pituitary surgery): give **desmopressin (DDAVP)** SC/IV/PO as per local policy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Strict 4-hourly urine output + fluid balance chart
  - Daily U&Es (more frequent if Na rising or falling rapidly)
  - If central DI: started on desmopressin, titrate to maintain normonatraemia and acceptable urine output (3-4 L/day)
  - If nephrogenic DI: stop offending drug (lithium), correct hypercalcaemia/hypokalaemia, consider thiazide diuretic or amiloride (nephrogenic DI)
  - Ensure free access to water; never restrict fluids in a conscious patient
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Central DI: desmopressin (oral, intranasal, or SC) — dose adjusted to thirst and serum Na
  - Nephrogenic DI: low-salt diet, thiazide diuretic (e.g., bendroflumethiazide 2.5-5 mg daily) ± amiloride
  - Endocrine follow-up for central DI; renal follow-up for nephrogenic
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 5% glucose/water at maintenance rate (e.g., 30 mL/kg/24h) + 0.9% saline for volume losses or if Na stable
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily unless contraindicated (weight, renal function)
  - Diet: normal with unrestricted salt (unless on thiazide)
  - Observations: 4-hourly – BP, HR, weight, urine output; NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (central DI):** Desmopressin (DDAVP) — dose: [check local policy] (typical: oral 100-200mcg TDS; IV/SC 1-2mcg BD; intranasal 10-20mcg daily)
- **Nephrogenic DI:** Thiazide diuretic — bendroflumethiazide 2.5-5 mg PO daily (check local policy); or amiloride 5-10 mg daily if lithium-induced
- **Key contraindications:** Desmopressin: caution in coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyponatraemia; thiazides: anuria, sulfonamide allergy, uncorrected hypokalaemia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Desmopressin – reduce dose in renal impairment; thiazides – avoid in severe renal impairment (eGFR < 30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Over-desmopressin causing hyponatraemia** — check Na daily; never repeat desmopressin without checking urine output and sodium. Signs: headache, nausea, confusion (hyponatraemic encephalopathy)

### Complications
- **Acute:** Severe hypernatraemia → seizures, cerebral oedema, coma; hypovolaemic shock; venous thrombosis from haemoconcentration
- **Chronic:** Hydronephrosis from massive urine flow (uncommon); bladder distension; renal impairment from repeated volume depletion; side effects of desmopressin (hyponatraemia)

### Prognosis
- Central DI: excellent with appropriate desmopressin replacement; quality of life good if compliant. Nephrogenic DI: reversible if cause corrected; if chronic (e.g., lithium), may need lifelong thiazide therapy; renal prognosis dependent on underlying condition.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Perform urine osmolality on first void** – baseline result guides water deprivation test decision
- **In any polyuria after pituitary surgery** – give a stat dose of desmopressin and monitor response; don’t wait for formal diagnosis
- **If patient has free access to water – they may correct hypernatraemia themselves** – chart fluid intake accurately

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not restrict water** – this causes rapid Na rise and cerebral injury
- **Do not give desmopressin before ruling out primary polydipsia** – can cause severe hyponatraemia
- **Do not correct hypernatraemia faster than 10 mmol/L per day** – risk of cerebral oedema and herniation

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Unable to maintain oral fluids (vomiting), new confusion, headache, seizure, or urine output > 5 L/day or < 500 mL/day
- **Follow-up:** Endocrine clinic in 2-4 weeks for central DI; renal clinic for nephrogenic DI; monitor serum Na weekly initially until stable

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - Na > 160 mmol/L or < 120 mmol/L
  - Seizures, altered GCS (< 15), focal neurological signs
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or lactate > 2 mmol/L
  - Anuria or oliguria (uncommon in DI)
- **Specific threshold:** GCS < 13 or rapid drop requires immediate senior review and consider ITU for controlled Na correction
